museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: March reveals PS2 and opens EverQuest.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for March 1999. Dates are deliberately conservative where regional timing is uncertain.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched month markers or context notes.

items.title: Sony reveals PlayStation 2 details on March 2.
items.body: A researched period marker, written cautiously where sources disagree or the month is more atmosphere than hard date.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates are used only when the reviewed sources support them.

items.title: EverQuest launches on March 16.
items.body: A month marker with regional timing kept visible rather than smoothed into a single global date.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates are used only when the reviewed sources support them.

items.title: Sega's Dreamcast suddenly has a larger shadow above it.
items.body: A researched period marker, written cautiously where sources disagree or the month is more atmosphere than hard date.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates are used only when the reviewed sources support them.

items.title: Persistent online worlds move from theory to subscription habit.
items.body: The network is becoming part of the exhibit, not just a footnote to boxed software.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates are used only when the reviewed sources support them.

items.title: The future now means both DVDs and online worlds.
items.body: The network is becoming part of the exhibit, not just a footnote to boxed software.
items.sourceNote: See source notes for this exhibit; exact dates are used only when the reviewed sources support them.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: EverQuest
items.body: Sony Online Entertainment makes a persistent fantasy world feel like an address.
items.sourceNote: 1999 in video games lists March 16, 1999.

items.title: Super Smash Bros.
items.body: Nintendo turns mascots into a party-fighting grammar in Japan.
items.sourceNote: Japanese release date is widely cited.

items.title: Driver
items.body: Reflections turns 1970s car-chase cinema into a mission-based city drive.
items.sourceNote: 1999 timing is secure; exact regional dates vary.

items.title: System Shock 2
items.body: Irrational and Looking Glass make a haunted spaceship out of RPG systems and fear.
items.sourceNote: August 1999 release is widely cited.

items.title: Final Fantasy VIII
items.body: Square's cinematic RPG reaches North America on the same date Dreamcast arrives there.
items.sourceNote: North American date is widely cited; Europe follows later.

items.title: Tony Hawk's Pro Skater
items.body: Neversoft makes skateboarding, punk licensing and score-chasing feel effortless.
items.sourceNote: Giant Bomb cites September 30, 1999; other lists cite September 29.

items.title: Age of Empires II: The Age of Kings
items.body: Ensemble turns medieval RTS into a LAN and skirmish staple.
items.sourceNote: September 1999 release is widely cited.

items.title: Sonic Adventure
items.body: Sega's mascot becomes a European Dreamcast launch symbol.
items.sourceNote: European Dreamcast launch context.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Machines, media and platform context around the month.

items.title: Sony reveals PlayStation 2 details
items.body: The PS2 announcement changes the weather around every console discussion.
items.sourceNote: Dreamcast sources cite March 2, 1999 PS2 reveal context.

items.title: Dreamcast reaches Europe
items.body: Sega's white console, VMU and modem arrive in the UK at £200.
items.sourceNote: Dreamcast sources cite October 14, 1999 Europe and £200 context.

items.title: Game Boy Color and Pokemon revive handheld shelves
items.body: In Britain, Pokemon makes old Game Boy habits feel suddenly current again.
items.sourceNote: European Pokemon Red/Blue October 1999 context.

items.title: PC online play becomes ordinary for enthusiasts
items.body: EverQuest, Battle.net, Unreal Tournament and Quake III make the internet feel like a gaming venue.
items.sourceNote: 1999 PC online context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How network play, downloads, fan pages and demo culture felt at the time.

items.title: EverQuest gives players a persistent address
items.body: Logging in can now mean returning to a world that kept moving without you.

items.title: Dreamcast ships with online intent
items.body: The modem turns console packaging into a promise about multiplayer, email and web access.

items.title: Fan sites and forums gather around secrets
items.body: Pokemon, Final Fantasy and Dreamcast coverage all grow in handmade online spaces.

items.title: LAN and modem culture divide PC players
items.body: Some games belong to bedrooms and phone lines; others to networked rooms and late nights.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading from the player side of history.

items.title: The century ended with a launch swirl
items.body: Dreamcast made Sega feel briefly new again, especially in the UK autumn.

items.title: Pokemon arrived as a social weather system
items.body: Trading, versions and rumours made Game Boy feel communal rather than obsolete.

items.title: PC games became places
items.body: EverQuest and arena shooters made online presence part of identity.

items.title: The PS2 shadow changed everything
items.body: Even before it launched, Sony's next machine made the future feel decided and contested at once.
